That entire comment block is more confusing/irrelevant than it is helpful, I think. I'd probably omit it entirely or, as you say, do away with the entire newtype.

> So I'm honestly not sure what the value of wrapping in an Arc<> is?

In this context there is no value, I think.

> It's already cheap to clone `&PyAny` as it simply increases Python's refcount, but that type only works when we are holding the GIL.

Copying and passing around `&PyAny` doesn't involve reference counts, it's a borrowed reference. Having it bound to the gil lifetime allows for cheap and safe working with borrowed references. But this struct needs to *own* a reference to it, which is what `Py<T>` represents.

> While we could clone the `PyObject` without the GIL, that would be an expensive deep clone.

Cloning a `Py<T>` without the GIL just increases a reference count (deferred into the future, when the gil is next acquired). No "deep copying" takes place.

> Seems like we're adding unnecessary locking.

`Arc` doesn't involve locking, just some math on atomic integers. Or I'm misunderstanding your comment.

there is already quite some code duplication regarding `urllib.request.Request(...` etc. This can be drastically improved by defining helper functions in the instrument class which just take the important part of the URL as an argument.

One direction that can be taken is to override `ask_raw` and `write_raw` methods of the instrument driver class, so that they take care of the `urllib` business, and prepending `self.parent.address` to the URL, and other common stuff. Once that is done, the code of `set_power` on this channel will look much simpler, smth along the following lines:
```python
def set_power(self, power):
    self.write(cmd='setpower', args=f'+p6{self.channel_id}={power}')
```
which is much sorter and more readable. The `urllib` business would be then located in one place, and `set_power` code is much simpler. Together, this reduces possibilities for typos and bugs.

Moreover, if later there will be need for other parameters (don't know if this instrument has more than just "power enabled" :) ), it will be very easy to do because the `urllib` business and even the format of the URL string is taken care of by the `ask_raw`/`write_raw` methods of the instrument driver.

Works on:
atom d2500 - only core0/1 temps are reported
mobile intel core i3-7 cpus (3-6 gen at least): package and core temps are reported
Ryzen 1/1.5 (Tdie is present).

The high-level context is that we're executing the `setup.py` of an sdist here and attempting to control its environment - injecting setuptools and (optionally) wheel into sys.path so that `bdist_egg` and (optionally) bdist_wheel work. As such we can't `python setup.py bdist_wheel` against their setup.py directly since we need to inject `sys.path.insert(...)` modifications (granted - another approach would be to use `PYTHONPATH` and not pass `-E` to the interpreter - but I'm sticking with the existing technique and better isolation here).

As to the exec - I'm not exactly sure why it was done this way. Declaring `__file__` before `exec` suffices to inject `__file__` into the exec'd code which I think could have been the only point - let the 3rdparty `setup.py` think its `setup.py` even though it's being slurped in as a subset of a virtual setup.py pumped through stdin.

There are two common conventions in Python-land:

A. Single quotes for "technical strings" (~ Ruby symbols) and double quotes for "human-readable strings" (~ could be translated)
B. Double quotes for everything (except perhaps strings containing double quotes, in order to avoid backslash-escaping)

By default `black` uses option B. I used to be a fan of option A; `black` made me realize it creates mental overhead for almost no practical benefits. I'm now a convert to option B aka "just don't think about it".

I can re-run `black` with the `-s` option which preserves the choice of single or double quotes if you'd like (and rebase the rest of the pull request, which is non-trivial work because there will be lots of conflicts).

Conclusion: just tell me if this convinces you to go with double quotes or if you'd like me to preserve the original quote style.

Transaction issue? No, it was encountered in all cases where you define your query and run it separately. Like this:
```
query = Event.all()
async with in_transaction():
    result = await query
```

Because `Event.all()` was called before transaction started - it fetched `self.model.db` and got not `TransactionWrapper`, but `DBClient`. And then, when `query` is `await`ed - it called `.execute_query()` on DBClient, not on `TransactionWrapper`, which results in running query outside transaction, so that brings errors in most cases.

azure\keyvault\administration\_models.py:204: error: Argument "value" to "KeyVaultSetting" has incompatible type "str"; expected "SettingValueType"  [arg-type]

I've done a _lot_ of searching online, but I'm not entirely sure why this has been coming up. The reason that makes the most sense is that a TypeVar `T` is expected to be the same actual type everywhere throughout the scope of `T`'s use.

Since KeyVaultSetting is a `Generic[SettingValueType]`, in the case where `self.value` ends up being a `bool`, I think mypy is upset that elsewhere `value` is being used as a `str`. Outside of using `cast`, I'm not sure how we get around this issue and indicate that the type can be converted at runtime

why not remove these codes?

All data processing frameworks will _mention_ their query/execution planner in their docs, since they want to emphasize optimized performance as one of their value-adds and the execution model may be relevant for debugging, setting performance expectations, etc. But that's different than requiring the user to think about their execution plan when using their APIs. The latter should only be done when required, for the sake of the typical tenets of good API design: information hiding, API should be as small/simple as possible, don't introduce unnecessary concepts, keep API concepts as high-level as possible, don't leak implementation details, etc.

This is all moot since the serialization being lineage-based seems to be a fundamental behavioral part of the API contract, so including the lineage concept in the serialization APIs seems like the best route.

>Another option I can think of is serialize_as/to_lazy_dataset / deserialize_from_lazy_dataset 

I'd push against that since this isn't correct in terms of what's supported, since lineage-based serialization is also supported for eager datasets. We track the lineage even when in eager mode, and when calling `ds.serialize_lineage()` for a dataset in eager mode, only the lineage will be serialized. The only requirement is that the dataset is **created** from external data, which we capture in the lineage as read tasks and is currently equivalent to lazy datasources.

Ah okay, no the issue is different: `to_thread.run_sync()` now _propagates_ the context forwards but not backwards:

```python
from contextvars import ContextVar
import anyio

ctx = ContextVar[str]("ctx")

async def async_func() -> None:
    assert ctx.set("bar")

def sync_func() -> None:
    assert ctx.set("foo")

async def main() -> None:
    await async_func()
    assert ctx.get() == "bar"
    await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(sync_func)
    assert ctx.get() == "foo"  # fails

anyio.run(main)
```

So there is still a subtle difference between a async and async endpoint. Side note: I think at some point before a 1.0 support for sync endpoints should be dropped, I think @tomchristie suggested this as well in the past via Gitter (Tom if you recall any of this conversation, or don't think it happened, please correct me if I am wrong).

1. My sense from reading about contenteditable is that it is probably overkill for this job. It's meant for richer text editing. We just want a numeric input. 2. From an accessibility/markup perspective, input elements are more recommended if you can: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/textbox_role. If you use contenteditable then you have to remember to put roll="textbox".

`SERVER_NAME` is always set locally, `HTTP_HOST` can be passed by nginx or some proxy, and should be set correctly.

`SERVER_PORT` right now is always the `effective_port` which is set to `/tmp/waitress.test.sock` or wherever the socket resides in the case of the unix socket... and is not correct. We also don't respect `X-Forwarded-Port` or anything else to allow that to be overridden.

https://github.com/Pylons/waitress/blob/4ce8f82082a76aa62148b6f3300d16ec4184202e/waitress/task.py#L547

There was even discussion about it on Web-SIG: https://mail.python.org/pipermail/web-sig/2012-January/005097.html

Right now gunicorn for example just appends an empty string for `SERVER_PORT` after trying to parse the `HTTP_HOST` header: https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/91974f0f44080fdd6f2885832d101474671c4b9b/gunicorn/http/wsgi.py#L175

And `SERVER_NAME` gets set to the path of the unix domain socket if the proxy doesn't send a `Host` header...

This is a new issue though, and we can open one to fix this, but using `unix` as the `SERVER_NAME` is IMHO, wrong.

- It plays nicely with pytest's `==` special casing.
- I really want to avoid making this too complicated.
- We have many errors, and only one of them depends on the Python version, so this `re.sub` likely won't turn into a bigger mess over time.
- When you see `if sys.version_info < (3, 9):` in the code, you immediately know that this is some kind of legacy thing that can likely be ignored.
- This is easy to clean up if we ever drop support for python <3.9.

What I meant is that I believe there are only two call sites where the behavior is determined by `FilesNotFoundBehavior`. Indeed, there are many `PathGlobs` usages, but the majority of them hardcode what the `GlobMatchErrorBehavior` should be, e.g.:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/63e70c17732058393f13221ecbefeae74125ddcb/src/python/pants/backend/python/lint/isort/rules.py#L94-L99

Precisely, anywhere that we request the `GlobMatchErrorBehavior` in a rule signature, we must add this workaround. I believe this is only the rules `hydrate_sources` and `hydrate_bundles`. (We used to use the singleton to determine the error behavior for unmatched file args, but always error now.)

```diff
diff --git a/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/graph.py b/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/graph.py

@@ -717,7 +717,11 @@ async def hydrate_sources(

     )
     snapshot = await Get[Snapshot](PathGlobs, path_globs)
     fileset_with_spec = _eager_fileset_with_spec(
@@ -740,7 +744,11 @@ async def hydrate_bundles(

         )

1. We deploy the contract on `chain_id == 1` and store `chain_id == 1` in the contract
2. A fork happens into `chain_id`s 1 and 231.
3. A user tries to use the smart contract on chain 231.

In this case, the smart contract still remembers `chain_id == 1` (that's the left hand side).  But `blockchain_service.network_id` points to 231 because now the user is trying to use chain_id 231.  So the check fails here.  If the proxy allows, the transaction would succeed onchain because on-chain we don't check that the `chain_id` is a particular value.

> Tagging #2760 and @jblespiau who is maybe more on the N bypasses side of the spectrum than I am.

The bypass I was suggesting in the past was mostly about not performing a *dynamic* look-up every time we do a C++ to Python or Python to C++ conversion. I don't really consider this a "bypass", but more of a cache (if we know the C++ Type "A" maps to a given Python type, no need to do the look-up in the hash-map every single time to retrieve the same object again and again. It's even more obvious when you convert an std::vector<A>, you will do as many look-ups as there are items in the vector. I was suggesting to add the keyword "static" on the look-up line, and you get a huge speed-up. The only possible issue is if people unregister and re-register new types on the same symbol, but I don't even think it's possible (you cannot unregister a symbol as far as I know).
Another difference with the current CL is that I was suggesting to improve the normal path, not an exceptional one.

Specifically for the current PR, I would probably need to think more, but I am thinking that:

- Exceptions are known to be slow in Python. Exceptions should be exceptional, and not be the standard way to return something. 
- Most of the time, after an exception, we crash.
- Given they are expected to be exceptional, why do we want to optimize it in the first place?

So I am wondering (a) why try to optimize something that should not happen in normal circumstances, (b) and also why, according to the benchmark, it has any impact on the non-raising path : if no exception is raised, it shouldn't change the runtime, right?

The above code parses major version from the output and is able to correctly handle case when the version is in format: `13devel`.
```python
def get_major_version():
    version = subprocess.check_output(['postgres', '--version']).decode()
    version = re.match('^[^\s]+ [^\s]+ (\d+)(\.(\d+))?', version)
    return '.'.join([version.group(1), version.group(3)]) if int(version.group(1)) < 10 else version.group(1)
```

This seems to be about how an organization's name appears in a reference when that org is an author. I think short name (when present) is preferred for the following reasons:
  * readability of the reference itself (assuming the org is well known by the short name)
  * usage in new bibxml (e.g., reference.IEEE 802-1AX.2020.xml) includes the abbrev attribute and it's preferred
```<organization abbrev="IEEE">Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers</organization>```

Whether or not to use an abbreviated name for orgs in references is not included in current style guidance. Over time, either has been used. FWIW, the RPC often uses only the abbreviated name. For example: rfc9182.notprepped.xml contains
```
<author><organization>ISO</organization></author> for [ISO10589]
<author><organization>IEEE</organization></author> for [IEEE802.1AX]
```

Yes, but the whole point of this requested change was to not do that... `AbstractDataclass` doesn't have anything to do with dataclasses, the only thing it does is prevent initialization of it's child classes, and of itself, there is no need for this class since we don't need to block initialization of these dataclasses, even though they are only base classes.

`abc.ABC` does work with dataclasses just as well as it does with regular classes, it prevents initialization if those classes have some abstract methods defined. There is no reason why this behavior wouldn't be sufficient for these classes, and introducing `AbstractDataclass` makes the code much more complex without almost any benefit to it.

@carver I've hit another road block here with the stateless `ConsensusAPI` concept. I'm sorry I didn't notice this earlier. Here it goes:

So, we were able to get rid of the `HeaderCache` because that could be replaced by going from `validate_seal(header)` to `validate_seal_extension(header, parents)`. I should also note that other clients don't necessarily have a header cache in their clique implementation and that this was just me trying to make things work with just `validate_seal` that only takes a single header.

But the snapshot cache is something different. Every Clique implementation out there has this (afaik) and I believe it is at the very heart of clique so I'm not sure how we would get around it.

It basically means: If we don't cache snapshots then with every single header that we validate, we will have to load all previous header from the database until we hit the most recent checkpoint header (every 30000 header for Görli). As you can imagine, this is super super slow.

As a band-aid fix I moved this to the class level but that obviously isn't an acceptable solution. I can't help myself but think we have to move at least parts this back to the chain level unless you have other clever ideas that I might not be seeing.

I want to note that if you feel like playing with this (you can run it with https://github.com/ethereum/trinity/pull/1196 as simple as `trinity --goerli --disable-tx-pool`) then I'm happy if you take this in any direction that gets us closer to a merge. I'm not trying to offload this to you, just saying that if you have ideas then you should not feel obligated to discuss them first if you think you could just take a stab on it and finish it up. The out of order tasks can be potentially very expensive to execute and mutate the state in undesirable way. Two examples in Serve:

## Serve Controller
1. `__init__` # reconstruct the state from internal kv upon restart
2. `start_control_loop()` # long running
3. `create_backend()` # state mutating and starting new actors, but we checkpoint the state to internal kv
4. `create_endpoint()` # state mutating but the state is checkpointed to internal kv.

After restart, we don't want to re-run task 3 and 4 because the state is already reconstructed. 

## Serve Replica (e.g. running ML models)
1. `__init__`
2. `handle_request` # takes longer to run
3. `handle_request` # finished
4. ...

After restart, we don't want to rerun the computation for task 3 but we do want to retry task 2. Additionally, re-running task 3 will slow down the replica and do unnecessary compute work. 

## Sidenote
I understand the library can adopt to any ray core semantic changes. but it does seem like completed actor task won't be executed is a good semantic guarantee.

This assigns the selected `API_NAME` as a class attribute of `QtModuleNotFoundError`, so the value will already be set whenever one of these Error objects is created. The definition of `QtModuleNotFoundError` becomes this:

```py
class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
    """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
    _msg = 'The {name} module was not found.'
    _msg_binding = '' # this attribute is assigned when API_NAME is identified
    _msg_extra = ''

    def __init__(self, *, name, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
        msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
        msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=_msg_binding, **msg_kwargs)
        super().__init__(msg, name=name)

# <snip>
# line ~255:

# Set display name of the Qt API
API_NAME = API_NAMES[API]

# set the selected binding for the error classes
QtModuleNotFoundError._msg_binding = API_NAME
```

Personally, I think this is worse than using `global` because this 'feature' is spread out across 200 lines. In the original, you only need to read the body of `QtModuleNotFoundError` to see how it works.

def get_average_of_unique_labels(labels, values):
    u, counts = np.unique(labels, return_counts = True)
    means = np.zeros( (len(u), *values.shape[1:]) )
    means[labels] += values
    #manually broadcast counts to have the same dimension as means
    means /= np.reshape(counts, (-1,) + (1,)*len(values.shape[1:]))

Assuming you are referring to the deallocation.  Python deallocation routines are special.  Because they trigger underneath everything else and with their own timing.  Therefore, dealloc must eat any exceptions that get triggered or it will result in a Python SystemError thrown at a random time.  Most of the time will be at the end of scope (and thus outside of the test case).   

To handle this properly during testing you will see me using ``def f(): do something;  f()``  which will trigger the action and force a clean up so that we can detect if the call triggers a SystemError inappropriately.  That is also the reason for all the ``assertRaisesRegex`` calls as I need to ensure we get the correct trigger.

Therefore the correct behavior is to trigger a fault and make sure that we DO NOT get a fault because if we do then we will trigger bad behavior in Python (interrupt GC or fail to free memory).

```pycon
>>> t = torch.empty(2, 3, 4)

torch.Size([2, 3, 4, 5])

NOTE:  Consider starting with a built in style using the --style argument.

   lowercase : vhdl keywords, signal names etc... are lowercase
```
... if `--style` or `--configuration` was not used.  Something to nudge the user in the right direction.

So instead of VSG providing a single opinion on what your code should look like and forcing the user to modify that opinion, VSG would provide no opinion and allow the user to pick one from those provided. @simonlingoogle you raise a good question but my initial point is different (not directly related to `NetifUnicastAddress` entries being same as the one added earlier (which in my opinion is fine)).

Note that `Ip6::Address` and `Ip6::NetifUnicastAddress` are two different types:
- The `DuaManager::GetDomainUnicastAddress()` is returning an `Ip6::Address *` 
- The fact that this pointer is derived from a `Ip6::NetifUnicastAddress` instance is an internal/private implementation of `DuaManager` class.
  - Note that `mDomainUnicastAddress` is a private member of `DuaManager`.
- So using this fact from another class (`Mle` here) is not good/safe.
- If in future for any reason the `DuaManager` implementation gets changed (e.g., we save the IP address differently), it would not be easy for developer making the change to realize that that `Ip6::Address *` returned from `GetDomainUnicastAddress()` may be used  like this in other modules.

----------

Regarding `Ip6::NetifUnicastAddress` and `Add/RemoveUnicastAddress()` (and multicast version), the fact that they add/remove the `NetifUnicastAddress` instance itself (and not copy it) is intentional and part of the design and public behavior of `Netif` class methods:
- We have two types of methods for adding/removing unicast addresses (similarly for multicast):
   -  `AddUnicastAddress()`/`RemoveUnicastAddress()`  (which are intended for core/internal use) and will directly use the passed-in `NetifUnicastAddress` instance itself.
   - `AddExternalUnicastAddress()`/`RemoveExternalUnicastAddress()` which will not use the passed-in `NetifUnicastAddress` instance and will create a copy of it.
- So if in other modules/classes, we rely on the fact that `Add/RemoveUnciastAddress()` is using the entry, it is consistent and part of the intended known public behavior of API/methods of `Netif` (though I would say, we have room for improvement in documenting this more clearly in the `Netif` module).

I'm not sure image urls order is really important, usually you end up with a directory of image files, and what matters is the file name; of course the image url order would matter if you used `num` to build a custom `filename_fmt`.

Anyway, even if we removed duplicates by preserving image url order in the `imageurls` list, at the moment this wouldn't reflect the order of image urls in the page, since `imageurls` is the product of two passes on the page, one for each search strategy (`imageurl_pattern_src` and `imageurl_pattern_ext`); but of course we could merge them into one big alternative regex and only make one pass (I originally divided it in two mainly for ease of testing).

So we have two options:
1. Status quo: don't care about image urls order
2. Refactor: merge imageurl search strategies into one regex, and remove duplicates preserving imageurl order

I would go for 1. for now, and leave 2. as an enhancement for the future

The API does not allow for metro + facility in the request, even if the facility is currently included in that metro.  

It's not enough to send empty strings for the opposite value. And sending `null` for the opposite value relies on undefined behavior.

The request that we send must either contain a non-empty facility or a non-empty metro or else the request is not valid according to the API. 

One of those fields (`facility`) was previously required, so now it has been made optional with a default value.

This client needs to accept both fields in the parameter list and omit one of those fields from the request. The user can't toggle that behavior with the param list alone, so the function needs logic to do it.

In Packngo we got this via:
```
type CreateDeviceInput struct {
 Facilities []string `json:"facilities,omitempty"`
 Metro string `json:"metro,omitempty"`
}
``` 

The conditional `params` setting is mimicking that behavior.

There are two "scenarios" where this `build_generic_metadata`/`update_generic_metadata_for_class` needs to do non-trivial work:

1. When you create a "new" generic class (i.e., when you create a new class that subclasses `BaseModel` _and_ `typing.Generic`). In this case, the `__pydantic_generic_metadata__` argument to `ModelMetaclass.__new__` will be `None`.
2. When you parametrize a generic class (i.e., call `BaseModel.__class_getitem__`), which in turn calls `create_generic_submodel`, this calls `ModelMetaclass.__new__` with a non-trivial `__pydantic_generic_metadata__`.

This implementation allows much/most of the logic to be shared between the two paths. I am not yet sure if this can be simplified; my intuition is it will require an annoying amount of logic duplication, but I'm looking into it.

Why don't we expand/rename `PydanticDecoratorMarker` into `DescriptorProxy`, add all of the relevant proxy methods (it's only 4 I think, maybe 30 LOC) and then use that everywhere? What we really want in all of these cases is for it to function as the original descriptor if accessed directly but hold extra metadata that we need when introspecting the model.

Something like this should do the trick:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Generic, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')


@dataclass
class DescriptorProxy(Generic[T]):
    wrapped: Any
    metadata: T

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        descriptor_methods = ('__get__', '__set__', '__delete__', '__set_name__')
        for method in descriptor_methods:
            if hasattr(self.wrapped, method):
                setattr(self, method, getattr(self.wrapped, method))
```

I checked the line and the error. I also played with the version that has no capacity check but it seems there is no check on the server-side. So, when you have a ring buffer with 10 elements capacity, if you make a `read_many` request with `min_count` larger than that, the server never sends back a response (waits to get at least `min_count` items even if it is higher than its capacity).

So, I guess we have to have some kind of capacity check to fail on bad user requests. I checked your implementation but it seems that even if the capacity check fails, the future returned to the user is not resolved with that failure. I tweaked it a little bit to do that. What do you think about it? I did some tests and it seems it is working.

```python

        check_not_negative(start_sequence, "sequence can't be smaller than 0")
        check_true(max_count >= min_count, "max count should be greater or equal to min count")
        check_true(max_count < MAX_BATCH_SIZE, "max count can't be greater than %d" % MAX_BATCH_SIZE)

        future = Future()

        def set_result(result):
            try:
                future.set_result(result.result())
            except:
                future.set_exception(sys.exc_info()[1], sys.exc_info()[2])

        def check_capacity(capacity):
            try:
                capacity = capacity.result()
                check_true(min_count <= capacity, "min count: %d should be smaller or equal to capacity: %d"
                           % (min_count, capacity))
                f = self._encode_invoke(ringbuffer_read_many_codec, response_handler=self._read_many_response_handler,
                                        start_sequence=start_sequence, min_count=min_count,
                                        max_count=max_count, filter=None)
                f.add_done_callback(set_result)
            except:
                future.set_exception(sys.exc_info()[1], sys.exc_info()[2])

        self.capacity().add_done_callback(check_capacity)

        return future

```

For explanation:

Unlike other configurations that can be defined in core config files, `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD` and `OPENTHREAD_RADIO` are special and are passed by the build system. Given that some other modules (like third_party) will also include the core config files but they are not built with FTD/MTD/RADIO definitions, this will cause some warnings. Because when building these modules, they will find the `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD`, `OPENTHREAD_RADIO` are not defined.

So we will avoid using `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD` and `OPENTHREAD_RADIO` in core config files. We will not use these options to define other options. For example, we won't define `CSL_TRANSMITTER` by `OPENTHREAD_FTD`. Instead, we will use `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD`, `OPENTHREAD_RADIO` together with the feature wrapper in the code. For example, `#if !OPENTHREAD_MTD && OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MAC_CSL_TRANSMITTER_ENABLE`. #5923 did the replacement.

There are potentially two use cases for checkpointing:
- further pre-train a bert model (say, the one provided by google) on a custom dataset using gluonnlp pre-training script, save a checkpoint, and then load it for fine-tuning classification/regression task. 
- run fine-tuning classification/regression for a few epochs, save a checkpoint, then resume fine-tuning from the checkpoint. 

Both are valid use case. However, for the second use case, we need to load both model parameter and trainer state (the mean and var in Adam) to resume training. 

I suggest we support the first use case here and explicitly document what the model parameters are for. For the second use case, usually fine-tuning is less expensive, there is less need to checkpoint and resume.

So, `futures_timer` _does_ have [a dedicated thread](https://docs.rs/futures-timer/0.1.1/futures_timer/#implementation-details), which means it isn't fork safe out of the box. To use it, you'd need to construct and provide the `Timer` reference manually (as described there), and ensure that it is shut down before forking.

An advantage to using `tokio_timer` (I think) is that it will "magically" use thread-local references to find the runtime it is running on, so you wouldn't need to pass in an explicit Runtime or Timer instance. The downside, of course, is that that is magical... you could fall into the trap I mentioned above with `io-pool` and see a panic due to it not being explicit.

I'm afraid I don't follow the reasoning here. Could you explain why this behavior is more hazardous in this context of a trivial module following a well-known common pattern, relative to the practical risk of having separate `console_scripts` and runpy entrypoints that may behave differently or get out of sync? The `if __name__ == "__main__"` idiom is very common (the [question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/419163/what-does-if-name-main-do) explaining how and why to use it is the second most popular tagged `python` on all of SO), especially in the scientific Python ecosystem, and is recommended in the [official Python docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/__main__.html) and countless other sources.

Further, this particular pattern is recommended a [substantial](https://chriswarrick.com/blog/2014/09/15/python-apps-the-right-way-entry_points-and-scripts/) [number](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27784271/how-can-i-use-setuptools-to-generate-a-console-scripts-entry-point-which-calls) of [places](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44977227/how-to-configure-main-py-init-py-and-setup-py-for-a-basic-package), none of which describe any such issues with it.

Of course, there is the general principle of separation of concerns, and thus that entry points and package functionality should be separate (which this still fully ensures, as this module contains no non-trivial functionality beyond that necessary to serve as a single-source entrypoint for both `console_scripts` and `runpy`). 

Beyond that, the only directly relevant post I found explicitly advocating against any use of `if __name__ == __main__` was [this personal blog](https://moshez.wordpress.com/2016/06/07/__name__-__main__-considered-harmful/), the one comment to which was a rebuttal by a Python core dev, and the (rather esoteric) potential harms mentioned don't apply to a module containing no non-trivial functionality beyond re-directing to the entry point. Some of these are at least theoretically possible if `qtpy,cli` were made runnable, but that's not what is being proposed here, and following your suggestions above, I rejected that. Therefore, I cannot see what the practical harm is, relative to the practical benefit (and avoidance of much more likely harm) of single-sourcing the entry piont.

I have been thinking about allowing a trailing slash path to this method, but I think it might cause some unexpected issues:

`DropboxClient.mkdir('/this/is/my/path/')` would work as the developer might expect (i.e. it creates the `/this/is/my/path`), but unfortunately `DropboxClient.exists('/this/is/my/path/')` would return False, which would surprise the developer.

If we decide to also strip the trailing slash in the exists() method, then `DropboxClient.exists('/files/existing_file.txt/')` would return True if the existing_file.txt exists (even if it is not a directory), which would surprise the developer.


I think that the current approach would be one of minimum surprise, and therefore a good one:
- Dropbox API does not allow the creation of paths ending with '/', so DropboxClient.mkdir('/') raises a ApiError due to `malformed_path` 
- Since no path ending with '/' can exist, `DropboxClient.exists(/existing/directory/')` returns false, and additionally logs a warning [_[link to code]_](https://github.com/joseignaciorc/luigi/blob/54586ed1d06a3576f53dd059e02ccbe736d00731/luigi/contrib/dropbox.py#L66 ). (Maybe it should be reworded? or changed into an exception to make it more obvious?).
- In DropboxTarget documentation, it is clearly stated that `The path must start with '/' and must not end with '/' (even if it is a directory).` [_[link to code]_](https://github.com/joseignaciorc/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/dropbox.py#L220).

This is one of the main motivations of using it in the first place — if you do want to write something high-performance, you'd write it in something like Rust and expose it as a Python C-extension. From an implementation standpoint, I was wondering whether we should use the name as the primary key or keep the usual integer and have a name field (with UNIQUE) like we do for labels for example: https://github.com/ansible-community/ara/blob/31f090e4119d1ac55a9807b9e605082cdd3db1b9/ara/api/models.py#L69

Having the name as a primary key provides the ability to do something like: ``/api/v1/distincthosts/<name>`` (rather than ``<id>``) which is nifty. This avoids needing to search for it like ``/api/v1/distincthosts?name=<name>`` and then recover the ID from the list, though the name search is partial so it could potentially return more than one result if the name search is a subset of another host name.

More importantly, it doesn't prevent us from searching by name anyway -- i.e, https://dev.demo.recordsansible.org/hosts?name=localhost returns localhost in addition to localhost1, localhost2, etc.

With that in mind, it's probably a good idea to keep name as the primary key.

I think it is fine to put it in the finally block. Maybe even better to.

The code as it is now will try the "except put/join" if f completes successfully but the first put/join sequence raises an exception.

Putting the put/join code in `finally`, with only the invocation of `f` in the try body, will behave differently: it will catch exceptions from invoking `f`, but will not catch exceptions from invoking put/join.

I think it is better to keep the try block as tightly focused around the invocation of `f` as possible.

To keep the p.terminate() in there even if put/join throws an exception, wrap the whole thing in a new `try/finally` block.

```
RESERVED_CHARACTERS = ':/?#[]@'

Then, we can modify the blacklist to be:

```
blacklist = [
    re.compile(
        r'://[^{}\s]+:([^{}\s]+)@'.format(
            re.escape(RESERVED_CHARACTERS + SUB_DELIMITER_CHARACTERS),
            re.escape(RESERVED_CHARACTERS + SUB_DELIMITER_CHARACTERS),

]

I couldn't see a case where this would somehow impact the expected behavior negatively. Having access to the request headers are one of the biggest use cases for using request callbacks for the user. Therefore, the reason this moved after headers was because an expected behavior is being able to access the headers before the request is sent out. for example a user's callback method would be:
```
def _callback(request):
            header = request.http_request.headers.get('x-header')
            if header == "original_value":
                    request.http_request.headers['x-header'] = "changed_value"
```

In this case if the request hook is before the headers policy this would always fail even though the header might be in fact set to `'original_value'`.
If we decide to move the request hook back then that means we would have to move the headers policy back. If we move the headers policy back then the outdated `x-ms-date` header issue would be back. 

So one of two solutions is possible here:
1. Leave the changes made in this PR (i.e, `StorageRequestHook` moved after the new `headers_policy` location)
2. Revert the changes of the headers policy and the storage request hook policy, but remove the `x-ms-date` header setting to be set in a policy after the retry policy (which would mean a non-header policy doing a header operation - which is not great)

sorry for replying so late, didn't have time for a couple days. I think I implemented it in this way because the fft + ifft may introduce image intensities outside the original data-type range. For example if you put in a uint8 image, you may get negative intensities. This is because when you remove some spatial frequencies, you might get "jumps" in places where there are edges. My original interpretation of `preserve_range` was that the output image should fit in the same dtype range as the input image so that it could also be cast as the same dtype. This is why I do the rescaling after the filter. But I now see your point that this has the potentially unwanted side effect of stretching the histogram to the extrema, so probably your solution is better. Note however that in your implementation, it is still possible to get an image that is not rescaled to a range [0, 1] or [-1, 1] if `preserve_range=False`, but I don't see another appropriate alternative that won't stretch the histogram. I suggest the following:

* I change the implementation to what @grlee77 suggests
* I add some clarification in the `Notes` section about `preserve_range` that it is possible to still get some outlier intensities
* I remove the test data files and hardcode new test data into the test files.

I implement the check for `None` because I got an error from the pytest. Apparently, there is a case with `None`. But I see now, that the implementation ist wrong :D I changed an `or` with an `is` and a `and` with an `or`:
```python
if mixed_result.stdev(param) is  None and numerical_result.stdev(param) is None:
    assert True
elif mixed_result.stdev(param) is None or numerical_result.stdev(param) is None:
    assert False
else:
    assert mixed_result.stdev(param) == pytest.approx(numerical_result.stdev(param))
```
I would prefer the following implementation, because if only one object is `None` the test has to fail. In your case, you would try to compare the object, if at least one of them is `None`. 
```python
if mixed_result.stdev(param) is not None and numerical_result.stdev(param) is not None:
    assert mixed_result.stdev(param) == pytest.approx(numerical_result.stdev(param))
else
    assert  mixed_result.stdev(param) is None and numerical_result.stdev(param) is None
```
or would you suggest skipping the test if more than one object is `None`?

The behavior is the same, but it avoids overhead of NumPy calls on short length 2 or 3 arrays. Originally this function was potentially being called for each value in the mask so it could add up over thousands of calls. After the loop reorganization, it is now only being called for mask locations that are near the boundary, so I doubt it is very critical to keep this change. 

Here is a short example:
```Python
radius = 2
nd_idx = np.asarray([64, 83])

%timeit tuple(max(p - radius, 0) for p in nd_idx)
# 1.48 µs ± 26 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit (nd_idx - radius).clip(min=0)
# 7.99 µs ± 151 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100000 loops each)
```
so the overhead is only around 6.5 microseconds per call on my machine, but it was being called thousands of times. Similar changes were also realized outside of the function as well since the function now returns tuples instead of small arrays.

In the current iteration of the PR, it was intentional to include Python 3.4 in the set of supported versions. My reasoning was that, since #21 uses the `enum` module, which is only included in Python 3.4 and up, we _know_ that the package won't work on 3.3 after that gets merged, whereas we don't have any similar reason to expect that it wouldn't work on 3.4. So I was thinking I'd just remove support for 3.3 in this PR, in anticipation of #21 getting merged later. That way, if anyone is still stuck using Python 3.4 and wants to install this package, they can still try their luck with it, whereas I think changing `python_requires` to exclude 3.4 would actually prevent it from being installed on that version.

Anyway, in retrospect, it probably does make more sense make it so that only 3.5+ is officially supported because those are the only versions we're testing, so I'm on board with that.

BTW maybe this is just me, but I'd prefer to express it as

```suggestion
    python_requires='>=3.5',
```

because if someone briefly glances at the entry and sees the version number but misses or misreads the comparison operator, then they'll come away with a version that the package actually does work on (3.5). Also, I'm not totally sure offhand if `>3.4` would allow 3.4.1 and so on. What do you think?

Without the `not self._messages` check, such a message would not be added to the batch, but still trigger a commit - of an empty batch.

I presume we want to accept such message and publish it immediately?

After all `max_bytes` is not the `byte_limit` setting from the upcoming publish flow controller, it's just a threshold to determine when to commit the batch.

There are two ratios, `r_max/L` and `sigma/r_max` that were somewhat unrealistic for practical use, AND the benchmark was taking too long.

Previously, `sigma=0.1` and `r_max=10` meant that `sigma/r_max` was very small, and the contributions near `r=r_max` were at the 100-sigma limit of a normal distribution (effectively zero). Changing that ratio to `sigma/r_max=10` is much more realistic. In practice it's fine to use an even lower value like 3, just depends on how precisely the integral over all grid spaces needs to get to 1. The tests in this PR explicitly check this normalization, which is related to issue #611.

The other relevant ratio is `r_max/L`, which was previously `3.1`. This meant that every point's Gaussian is contributing to (and being computed for) an extremely large proportion of grid cells. Like `4/3 * pi * (1/3.1)**3 = 0.1406`. That's not really how I've seen GaussianDensity used. I assumed a system of 10x10x10 particles in a simple cubic lattice, where every particle has a diameter of 1 (radius 0.5, diameter is equivalent to 10 bin widths since `width=100`).

Okay, I am on board with the argument for making it a bit more work to store nonpersistent state in instance attributes. (It helps that there are a number of existing tasks where I think instance attributes are overused.)

But you didn't answer the other part of my question, about the possibility of keeping persistent state more explicitly contained in a separate `state` object. My arguments for this are:
- It makes it clearer which values are being persisted; instead of having to keep track of which names are being stored which way, you can just look at where the value is being set.
- It would remove the need to manage a list of excluded fields for each task, and clean up the ResumableTask implementation a bit since it wouldn't need to filter based on what's in excluded_fields
- It makes resuming more straightforward: The task can work with a state object reconstructed in its entirety from the resumption file, instead of needing to transfer specific state values to specific task attributes.

and my async version:
```

In the first example you are sending the read command, and then execute check_error directly afterwards. No check if AsReadArea was received.

In my example you are sending the request, wait until a timeout/execution and then check_error.

This leads to two different `results` (one checked/legit result, one not checked/faulty/perhaps right result)  and so `check_error()` may return different results, wouldn't it? 
I want explicitly wait until `CheckAsCompletion()` shows green light for a valid result, before I want to execute check_error(), because your result and my result will mostly be different.

If I would just make a decorator at `as_area_read()`,  the first `check_error()` may throw a Snap7Exception, even if I wait until a legit result afterwards in my wrapper code and the second `check_error()` there would be fine with that.

Chatted offline about this.  The thing that I was hoping to improve was to unify the configuration of the `WebsocketAPI` because you have to do both `websocket_api.session = Session()` as well as `websocket_api.configure(..., ...)` to get the object to a point where `send()` will work correctly.  I get why it's two steps, the `configure()` call actually happens in the chalice runtime, but it would be nice to consolidate.

However, there's the more general question about whether we should use boto3 session's directly.  My take on it is that it seems like a reasonable middle ground for the time being, but I think you're right about people wanting more control over the clients and how they get instantiated.  Either that or they want us to take care of everything.

John had an interesting idea about how chalice could have better automatic support with boto3.  We don't include it right now because it's a large dependency, especially if you don't need it.  A default chalice app is a couple of small .py files, which is nice.  However, what if we know you *do* need it?

The chalice mentality is to do things for the user they we know we need to do, but let the override it if they want (e.g. policy generation).  So one idea (and it's just an idea for now) is to detect if the user needs boto3, and if they do, to just _make it work_.  For example, if they have websocket routes defined, and they're calling `websock_api.send()`, then you _must_ have a boto3 client to do that.  So when we detect that we can check:

* Is boto3 in the deployment package?  If not, automatically include it for them.
* Is there a boto3 `_client` on the websocket api?  If not create one for them.

At any point the user can override this (add boto3 to their requirements.txt file), or manually set a client on the websocket API, but by default we can just try to do the right thing for them.  Obviously there's a lot of details and edge cases to work out, but I think it's a really interesting idea.

For the meantime, given this is an experimental API, as long as we clearly document that this API might change I'm ok with keeping this for now, but I'm interested to hear any alternatives if you have them.

Agreed. I don't expect consumers to be relying on a specific exception. I'm less concerned with changing the exception than I am about the exception changing depending on the optimization flags. One of the features I like about Python is that you don't have to double-check every conditional at run-time. I like writing the minimal amount of code to achieve the primary purpose and hope that exceptions clearly indicate the proximate cause of exceptional conditions. The existing implementation does exactly what I'd want - it _assumes_ the type is a match and fails otherwise. In this case, the assert adds noise to the implementation and runtime variability in the interface.

I don't like how `mypy` is making us second-guess ourselves here. This issue occurs in other places in the code where the need for an assertion causes the need to introduce new scopes of execution (replacing an expression with multiple lines of execution adding names to the local namespace).

Probably the right thing to do here is for Python's regex object to grow a new method (e.g. `ensure_match` or similar) that unconditionally returns a match or raises an exception if no match is found.

Lacking that functionality, perhaps this project should supply that functionality (provide a wrapper that ensures that interface). Such a wrapper could also raise an exception with extra context about what didn't match on what input.

Alternatively, this assertion could be couched in an `if typechecking` block so it's clear that it's typing noise and not essential to the implementation.

```suggestion
        if typing.TYPE_CHECKING:
            assert match is not None
```

On further consideration, I don't like that approach because it still requires breaking out a simple expression into a block of code. I'd say that any solution should be implemented as a wrapper function that's usable in an expression.

So we do add +1. The reason we have to modify threshold is because if the size (so total number of elements in the array) of the array is less than the threshold, it will just print the entire array. We need to reduce the threshold to get the `...` numbers if the array is bigger than the threshold.

E.g. the default threshold is 1000. If we have a 2000 long array, and want 3 edge items, we'd get 49 items, and try to repr that. But when we try and get the repr of the 49 items, NumPy thinks that we're trying to repr an array of size 49, so it prints the whole thing out. We need to modify the repr so NumPy still prints the `...` etc. even though it thinks its actually dealing with a relatively smaller array.

To get the `...`, we do the logic above, where if the axis is long enough, we get 0...num_elements and len(self)-num_elements...num_elements elements.

> Hm isn't either way compatible with eager?

It is, but it does this by creating "non-eager" tensors on which it attaches a hidden trace. My main issue is that it is a regression towards some magic hidden state. This seems to do something very similar to what `tf.function` does but in a much less explicit way. Decorating a function with `tf.function` clearly labels the part of the code that is traced, while I find this syntax doesn't delineated very clearly which tensors are actually eager and which ones are not. I could see this style be more error prone once the majority of users get used to TF eager/2.0 and more complex to mix and match with non-keras code.

> I like the existing style since it seems more explicit what is going on, for example I'm not sure what is going on when you use tf.keras.Sequential, it seems magical that it can create that without knowing the input shapes.

If you provide an input layer or a layer with its input dimensions defined, I think the variables are constructed right away. In this particular example, the shapes would be inferred the same way in either implementation. However, the fully OO way allows for variable creation to be deferred until the first call at which time the shapes can be inferred before they are needed.

The logic for that isn't very complex and is the recommended keras API for [custom layers](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/keras/layers/Layer). It just follows something along the lines of:
```python
def __call__(self, inp):
    if not self._built:
        self.create_variables(inp)
    self.call(inp)
```

That being said, since this is just an example, I don't see any issues with leaving it as is if that is what you prefer.

There seems some issue for DUA prefix since it's not always OFF mesh reachable:
- DUA prefix with `mSlaac=0` can't address 1.1 devices
- DUA prefix with `mSlaac=1` can address 1.1 devices, but these DUAs are not registered, thus can't be properly ND proxyed

So, if we say `mSlaac=0` DUA prefix should not be considered as OMR, then neither should `mSlaac=1` DUA prefix, since they are both problematic. So, I think we should choose either 1. all DUA prefixes are OMR, 2. DUA prefix is never OMR.

I did a bit of digging here and `inspect.iscoroutinefunction()` changes the type to `(...) -> types.CoroutineType[Any, Any, Any]` which is not compatible with `FT` (bound to an `Awaitable[]` return). There are two ways to fix this depending on what the goal is:

* Change `LF`, `FT`, and `ET` to be bound to a callable that returns `Coroutine[Any, Any, Any]`. If we want the code to stay the same as it is right now, this is the correct solution.
* Change the task code to check if the return value of the functions is awaitable rather than checking if the function itself is a coroutine function. If we want `Loop` to be able to use functions returning awaitables, this is the correct solution.

Returning a `static_cast` of the parent's operator is unnecessary compared to `return *this`. Any compiler should be able to able to optimize away the `*this` operation.

More important, this may introduce undefined behavior. Specifically, there is no guarantee that the parent `=` operator will return `this`; it is done by convention but not required. In case the operator returns something else, we are now statically downcasting an unknown pointer to a type it may not be, and one which we may not own.

So here's the funny thing -- according to [this](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/debug/system-error-codes--0-499-)

Error 32 on Windows is `ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION`: "The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process." This makes the most sense for the original code of the `if` statement here.

Error 13 is `ERROR_INVALID_DATA` on Windows which might be the correct one for "file expected" but I'm not sure, given there are things like `ERROR_DIRECTORY_NOT_SUPPORTED`.

_However_

POSIX error code 13 is `EACCES`, which is what you'd expect from a failed access due to a file being locked. So it looks like we should be checking `winerror` against 32, not errno?

```py
windows_error = getattr(exc_value, "winerror", 0)
exception_args = getattr(exc_value, "args", None) or (0,)
if windows_error == 32 or exception_args[0] == errno.EACCES:
    # proper exception type is errors.ResourceLocked
```

We use the build root for both `run` and `repl` because we want to give users the flexibility of accessing the build root. For example, `run` needs to be able to write files to the build root, without forcing users to have their scripts use absolute paths to the build root. Your new `ReplRequest.run_in_workspace` field is a good one though, and I think here in `core/goals/repl.py` you will want to use an `if` statement: if running in workspace, use the temp dir stuff, unmodified from before. ```

it should be 
```
options = webdriver.ChromeOptions()
options.method("arg:param")
